steps.title: Check whether the equations are multiples of each other
steps.content: Given: \(3.5x + 1.2y = 8\) and \(17.5x + 6y = 40\). Multiply every term of the first equation by 5: $$5(3.5x) = 17.5x,\quad 5(1.2y) = 6y,\quad 5(8) = 40$$ The result is exactly \(17.5x + 6y = 40\) — the second equation.

steps.title: Test all four choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Exactly one': A unique solution requires non-parallel, non-identical lines. These equations are identical after scaling, so they define the same line — infinitely many shared points. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Exactly two': Two linear equations cannot share exactly two points; if they share more than one they are the same line. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Infinitely many': Since the ratios \(\frac{17.5}{3.5} = \frac{6}{1.2} = \frac{40}{8} = 5\) are all equal, the equations are equivalent — every point on one line satisfies the other. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Zero': Zero solutions requires parallel but distinct lines (equal coefficient ratios, unequal constant ratio). Here all three ratios are equal, so the lines are identical, not parallel-distinct. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
